toolUseResult.newString: z_fmt="${z_fmt//%b/%s}"
toolUseResult.originalFile:
#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2025 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# Bash Utility Handbook - Always-visible user interaction output
#
# This module provides formatted output for interactive procedures where
# the user MUST see the output to proceed (OAuth flows, manual steps, etc).
# All output goes to stderr and is NOT subject to verbosity control.
#
# Function naming convention encodes color sequence for positional args:
#   t = text (default color)
#   c = command/code (cyan)
#   u = UI element (magenta) - text user sees on screen
#   W = warning (yellow) - uppercase for visual pop
#   E = error (red) - uppercase for visual pop
#   l = link (blue underline, OSC-8 hyperlink) - consumes TWO args
#
# Each letter consumes one positional argument, except 'l' which consumes
# two (display_text then url). This is the only multi-arg letter.
#
# Example: buh_tut "Click the " "Save" " button"
#   Renders: "Click the " + magenta("Save") + " button"
#
# Link arg mapping example for buh_tlt:
#   buh_tlt  "A "  "hallmark"  "https://...#hallmark"  " is a named artifact."
#            ^^^^  ^^^^^^^^^^  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^   ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
#            t(1)  l(1:text)   l(2:url)                t(1)

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion guard
test -z "${ZBUH_INCLUDED:-}" || return 0
ZBUH_INCLUDED=1

######################################################################
# Internal: Kindle and Sentinel

zbuh_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZBUH_KINDLED:-}" || return 0

  # Color support detection
  local z_use_color=0
  if test -z "${NO_COLOR:-}" && test -n "${TERM:-}" && test "${TERM}" != "dumb"; then
    z_use_color=1
  fi

  if test "${z_use_color}" = "1"; then
    readonly ZBUH_R="\033[0m"          # Reset
    readonly ZBUH_T=""                 # Text (default - no color change)
    readonly ZBUH_C="\033[36m"         # Command (cyan)
    readonly ZBUH_U="\033[35m"         # UI element (magenta)
    readonly ZBUH_W="\033[1;33m"       # Warning (bright yellow)
    readonly ZBUH_E="\033[1;31m"       # Error (bright red)
    readonly ZBUH_S="\033[1;37m"       # Section (bright white)
    readonly ZBUH_L="\033[34;4m"       # Link (blue + underline)
  else
    readonly ZBUH_R=""
    readonly ZBUH_T=""
    readonly ZBUH_C=""
    readonly ZBUH_U=""
    readonly ZBUH_W=""
    readonly ZBUH_E=""
    readonly ZBUH_S=""
    readonly ZBUH_L=""
  fi

  # Mutable kindle state — step counters, body indent, step format
  z_buh_step1_n=0
  z_buh_step2_n=0
  z_buh_body_indent=""
  z_buh_step_prefix=""
  z_buh_step_separator=". "

  readonly ZBUH_KINDLED=1
}

zbuh_sentinel() {
  test "${ZBUH_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || { zbuh_kindle; }
}

######################################################################
# Internal: Core output

zbuh_show() {
  zbuh_sentinel
  printf '%b\n' "${z_buh_body_indent}${1:-}" >&2
}

######################################################################
# Internal: Link fragment builder
#
# Sets ZBUH_LINK_FRAG to a formatted link fragment for embedding in
# combinator output.  Respects BURD_NO_HYPERLINKS fallback.
# Args: display_text url

zbuh_link_fragment() {
  zbuh_sentinel
  local -r z_text="${1:-}"
  local -r z_url="${2:-}"
  if test -n "${BURD_NO_HYPERLINKS:-}"; then
    ZBUH_LINK_FRAG="${ZBUH_L}${z_text}${ZBUH_R} <${z_url}>"
  else
    ZBUH_LINK_FRAG="${ZBUH_L}\033]8;;${z_url}\033\\\\${z_text}\033]8;;\033\\\\${ZBUH_R}"
  fi
}

######################################################################
# Public: Section headers and numbered steps

buh_section() { zbuh_sentinel; z_buh_body_indent=""; printf '%b\n' "${ZBUH_S}${1}${ZBUH_R}" >&2; }
buh_e()       { echo "" >&2; }

buh_step_style() {
  zbuh_sentinel
  z_buh_step_prefix="${1:-}"
  z_buh_step_separator="${2:-". "}"
}

buh_step1() {
  zbuh_sentinel
  z_buh_step1_n=$((z_buh_step1_n + 1))
  z_buh_step2_n=0
  z_buh_body_indent="   "
  printf '%b\n' "${ZBUH_S}${z_buh_step_prefix}${z_buh_step1_n}${z_buh_step_separator}${1}${ZBUH_R}" >&2
}

buh_step2() {
  zbuh_sentinel
  z_buh_step2_n=$((z_buh_step2_n + 1))
  z_buh_body_indent="      "
  printf '%b\n' "   ${ZBUH_S}${z_buh_step_prefix}${z_buh_step1_n}.${z_buh_step2_n}${z_buh_step_separator}${1}${ZBUH_R}" >&2
}

######################################################################
# Public: Text combinators
#
# Naming: sequence of t/c/u/W/E describes positional arg colors
# Each letter consumes one positional argument

# Single element (sorted)
buh_c()       { zbuh_show "${ZBUH_C}${1}${ZBUH_R}"; }
buh_E()       { zbuh_show "${ZBUH_E}${1}${ZBUH_R}"; }
buh_t()       { zbuh_show "${1}"; }
buh_u()       { zbuh_show "${ZBUH_U}${1}${ZBUH_R}"; }
buh_W()       { zbuh_show "${ZBUH_W}${1}${ZBUH_R}"; }

# Two elements (sorted)
buh_ct()      { zbuh_show "${ZBUH_C}${1}${ZBUH_R}${2}"; }
buh_tc()      { zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_C}${2}${ZBUH_R}"; }
buh_tE()      { zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_E}${2}${ZBUH_R}"; }
buh_tu()      { zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_U}${2}${ZBUH_R}"; }
buh_tW()      { zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_W}${2}${ZBUH_R}"; }
buh_ut()      { zbuh_show "${ZBUH_U}${1}${ZBUH_R}${2}"; }

# Three elements (sorted)
buh_tct()     { zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_C}${2}${ZBUH_R}${3}"; }
buh_tcu()     { zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_C}${2}${ZBUH_R}${ZBUH_U}${3}${ZBUH_R}"; }
buh_tEt()     { zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_E}${2}${ZBUH_R}${3}"; }
buh_tuc()     { zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_U}${2}${ZBUH_R}${ZBUH_C}${3}${ZBUH_R}"; }
buh_tut()     { zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_U}${2}${ZBUH_R}${3}"; }
buh_tWt()     { zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_W}${2}${ZBUH_R}${3}"; }

# Four elements (sorted)
buh_tctc()    { zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_C}${2}${ZBUH_R}${3}${ZBUH_C}${4}${ZBUH_R}"; }
buh_tctu()    { zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_C}${2}${ZBUH_R}${3}${ZBUH_U}${4}${ZBUH_R}"; }
buh_tcut()    { zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_C}${2}${ZBUH_R}${ZBUH_U}${3}${ZBUH_R}${4}"; }
buh_tuct()    { zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_U}${2}${ZBUH_R}${ZBUH_C}${3}${ZBUH_R}${4}"; }
buh_tutE()    { zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_U}${2}${ZBUH_R}${3}${ZBUH_E}${4}${ZBUH_R}"; }
buh_tutu()    { zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_U}${2}${ZBUH_R}${3}${ZBUH_U}${4}${ZBUH_R}"; }

# Five elements (sorted)
buh_tctct()   { zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_C}${2}${ZBUH_R}${3}${ZBUH_C}${4}${ZBUH_R}${5}"; }
buh_tutut()   { zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_U}${2}${ZBUH_R}${3}${ZBUH_U}${4}${ZBUH_R}${5}"; }

######################################################################
# Public: Hyperlinks
#
# buh_link "prefix text" "link text" "url" "suffix text"
# Renders clickable hyperlink with OSC-8 escape sequences

buh_link() {
  zbuh_sentinel
  local z_prefix="${1:-}"
  local z_text="${2:-}"
  local z_url="${3:-}"
  local z_suffix="${4:-}"

  # Blue + underline style
  local z_link_style="\033[34;4m"

  if test -n "${BURD_NO_HYPERLINKS:-}"; then
    # Fallback: styled text with URL in angle brackets
    printf '%s%s%b%s%b <%s>%s\n' \
      "${z_buh_body_indent}" "${z_prefix}" "${z_link_style}" "${z_text}" "${ZBUH_R}" "${z_url}" "${z_suffix}" >&2
  else
    # OSC-8 hyperlink with styling
    printf '%s%s%b\033]8;;%s\033\\%s\033]8;;\033\\%b%s\n' \
      "${z_buh_body_indent}" "${z_prefix}" "${z_link_style}" "${z_url}" "${z_text}" "${ZBUH_R}" "${z_suffix}" >&2
  fi
}

######################################################################
# Public: Link combinators
#
# The 'l' letter consumes TWO positional args (display_text, url) and
# renders an OSC-8 terminal hyperlink.  All other letters consume one.
#
# Arg mapping example for buh_tlt:
#   buh_tlt  "A "  "hallmark"  "https://...#hallmark"  " is a named artifact."
#            ^^^^  ^^^^^^^^^^  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^   ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
#            t(1)  l(1:text)   l(2:url)                t(1)

buh_lt()      { zbuh_link_fragment "${1}" "${2}"; zbuh_show "${ZBUH_LINK_FRAG}${3}"; }
buh_tl()      { zbuh_link_fragment "${2}" "${3}"; zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_LINK_FRAG}"; }
buh_tlt()     { zbuh_link_fragment "${2}" "${3}"; zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_LINK_FRAG}${4}"; }
buh_tlc()     { zbuh_link_fragment "${2}" "${3}"; zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_LINK_FRAG}${ZBUH_C}${4}${ZBUH_R}"; }

buh_tltlt() {
  zbuh_link_fragment "${2}" "${3}"
  local -r z_frag1="${ZBUH_LINK_FRAG}"
  zbuh_link_fragment "${5}" "${6}"
  zbuh_show "${1}${z_frag1}${4}${ZBUH_LINK_FRAG}${7}"
}

buh_tltltlt() {
  zbuh_link_fragment "${2}" "${3}"
  local -r z_frag1="${ZBUH_LINK_FRAG}"
  zbuh_link_fragment "${5}" "${6}"
  local -r z_frag2="${ZBUH_LINK_FRAG}"
  zbuh_link_fragment "${8}" "${9}"
  zbuh_show "${1}${z_frag1}${4}${z_frag2}${7}${ZBUH_LINK_FRAG}${10}"
}

buh_tltltltlt() {
  zbuh_link_fragment "${2}" "${3}"
  local -r z_frag1="${ZBUH_LINK_FRAG}"
  zbuh_link_fragment "${5}" "${6}"
  local -r z_frag2="${ZBUH_LINK_FRAG}"
  zbuh_link_fragment "${8}" "${9}"
  local -r z_frag3="${ZBUH_LINK_FRAG}"
  zbuh_link_fragment "${11}" "${12}"
  zbuh_show "${1}${z_frag1}${4}${z_frag2}${7}${z_frag3}${10}${ZBUH_LINK_FRAG}${13}"
}

buh_tltTtT() {
  zbuh_link_fragment "${2}" "${3}"
  local -r z_frag1="${ZBUH_LINK_FRAG}"
  zbuh_tabtarget_fragment "${5}"
  local -r z_frag2="${ZBUH_TT_FRAG}"
  zbuh_tabtarget_fragment "${7}"
  zbuh_show "${1}${z_frag1}${4}${z_frag2}${6}${ZBUH_TT_FRAG}${8}"
}

######################################################################
# Internal: Tabtarget fragment builder
#
# Sets ZBUH_TT_FRAG to a resolved tabtarget path in cyan for embedding
# in combinator output.  Resolves colophon via glob.
# Args: colophon

zbuh_tabtarget_fragment() {
  zbuh_sentinel
  local z_matches=("${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}"/${1}.*)
  test -e "${z_matches[0]}" || buc_die "buh: no tabtarget for colophon '${1}'"
  ZBUH_TT_FRAG="${ZBUH_C}${z_matches[0]}${ZBUH_R}"
}

# Imprint tabtarget fragment: resolves colophon + imprint to a specific
# imprint tabtarget.  Glob: ${colophon}.*.${imprint}.sh
# Args: colophon imprint

zbuh_imprint_fragment() {
  zbuh_sentinel
  local z_matches=("${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}"/${1}.*.${2}.sh)
  test -e "${z_matches[0]}" || buc_die "buh: no tabtarget for colophon '${1}' imprint '${2}'"
  ZBUH_TT_FRAG="${ZBUH_C}${z_matches[0]}${ZBUH_R}"
}

######################################################################
# Public: Tabtarget combinators
#
# T consumes one positional arg (colophon), resolves to tabtarget path,
# renders in cyan.  Parallel to l (link) combinator.
# I consumes two positional args (colophon, imprint), resolves to a
# specific imprint tabtarget.
# Trailing c after T or I appends cyan text (arguments) to the resolved
# path, keeping the whole command visually unified.

buh_T()       { zbuh_tabtarget_fragment "${1}"; zbuh_show "${ZBUH_TT_FRAG}"; }
buh_tT()      { zbuh_tabtarget_fragment "${2}"; zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_TT_FRAG}"; }
buh_tTc()     { zbuh_tabtarget_fragment "${2}"; zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_TT_FRAG}${ZBUH_C}${3}${ZBUH_R}"; }
buh_tI()      { zbuh_imprint_fragment "${2}" "${3}"; zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_TT_FRAG}"; }
buh_tIc()     { zbuh_imprint_fragment "${2}" "${3}"; zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_TT_FRAG}${ZBUH_C}${4}${ZBUH_R}"; }
buh_cT()      { zbuh_tabtarget_fragment "${2}"; zbuh_show "${ZBUH_C}${1}${ZBUH_R}${ZBUH_TT_FRAG}"; }
buh_tlT()     { zbuh_link_fragment "${2}" "${3}"; zbuh_tabtarget_fragment "${4}"; zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_LINK_FRAG} ${ZBUH_TT_FRAG}"; }
buh_tltT()    { zbuh_link_fragment "${2}" "${3}"; zbuh_tabtarget_fragment "${5}"; zbuh_show "${1}${ZBUH_LINK_FRAG}${4}${ZBUH_TT_FRAG}"; }

######################################################################
# Public: Index renderers
#
# Group-level handbook indexes shared across kits.
# Each function renders one kit's handbook group tops.

buh_index_buk() {
  buh_section  "Generic OS Procedures (Bash Utility Kit)"
  buh_t        "  Project-independent OS-level mechanisms."
  buh_tT       "  Windows:  " "buw-hw"
}

######################################################################
# Public: Pre-composed line output
#
# buh_line format_string [args...]
#   Indent-aware (respects z_buh_body_indent from step context).
#   Interprets %b escapes — designed for lines pre-composed from
#   yelp capture fragments:
#     buh_line "A %b is where images live." "${RBYC_DEPOT}"

buh_line() {
  zbuh_sentinel
  local z_fmt="${z_buh_body_indent}${1}"
  # Yelp fragments are pre-rendered (literal ESC bytes, not \033 strings).
  # Map %b → %s to avoid double-interpretation — printf %b would misread
  # the OSC-8 ST sequence's \ followed by content (e.g., \c = cease output).
  z_fmt="${z_fmt//'%b'/'%s'}"
  # shellcheck disable=SC2059
  printf "${z_fmt}\n" "${@:2}" >&2
}

######################################################################
# Public: User prompts

# buh_prompt "prompt text"
# Displays prompt and reads user input, returns via stdout
buh_prompt() {
  zbuh_sentinel
  printf '%s' "${1:-}" >&2
  local z_input
  read -r z_input
  printf '%s' "${z_input}"
}

# buh_prompt_required "prompt text" "error message"
# Like buh_prompt but dies if input is empty
buh_prompt_required() {
  local z_input
  z_input=$(buh_prompt "${1:-}")
  if test -z "${z_input}"; then
    zbuh_show "${ZBUH_E}ERROR:${ZBUH_R} ${2:-Input required}"
    return 1
  fi
  printf '%s' "${z_input}"
}

######################################################################
# Public: Critical warnings (box format)

buh_critical() {
  zbuh_sentinel
  zbuh_show ""
  zbuh_show "${ZBUH_E}===============================================${ZBUH_R}"
  zbuh_show "${ZBUH_E}  CRITICAL: ${1}${ZBUH_R}"
  zbuh_show "${ZBUH_E}===============================================${ZBUH_R}"
  zbuh_show ""
}

# eof

lines: # Yelp fragments are pre-rendered (literal ESC bytes, not \033 strings).
# Map %b → %s to avoid double-interpretation — printf %b would misread
# the OSC-8 ST sequence's \ followed by content (e.g., \c = cease output).

input.description: Run First Crucible handbook after quote fix

content.content:
[1;37mStart a Crucible Using Local Builds[0m

A [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Crucible\Crucible]8;;\[0m is a sandboxed container environment with enforced
network isolation. You are going to build one on your workstation
and run Claude Code inside it — no cloud account, no credentials
beyond your own Claude subscription.

This track uses the [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#ccyolo\ccyolo]8;;\[0m [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Nameplate\Nameplate]8;;\[0m: a Claude Code sandbox that can
only reach Anthropic. Everything else is blocked.

Prerequisite: a Claude OAuth subscription (you will authenticate
inside the container via copy/paste from your browser).

[1;37mStep 1 — Build images locally[0m

   A [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Vessel\Vessel]8;;\[0m is a specification for a container image — a Dockerfile
   and build context. A [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Hallmark\Hallmark]8;;\[0m is a specific build instance,
   identified by a timestamp tag.

   [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Kludge\Kludge]8;;\[0m builds a vessel image locally using Docker — no cloud, no
   registry, no credentials. The fastest path from Dockerfile to running
   container without cloud image backup.

   [1;37mStep 1.1 — Kludge the sentry[0m

      The [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Sentry\Sentry]8;;\[0m is the gatekeeper container. It runs iptables
      and dnsmasq to enforce network policy — only domains you whitelist
      are reachable from inside.

      Kludge it:

         [36mtt/rbw-fk.LocalKludge.sh[0m[36m rbev-sentry-deb-tether[0m

      The output prints a hallmark tag — something like
      [36mk260413155458-f9dcb6d9[0m
      Copy it. You will paste it into the nameplate in Step 2.

       [ ] No kludge-tagged sentry image found

      [1;33mKludge requires a clean git tree.[0m
      If you have uncommitted changes, commit them first.  This is by
      design — container images not backed by git commits easy to confuse.

   [1;37mStep 1.2 — Kludge the bottle[0m

      The [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bottle\Bottle]8;;\[0m is your workload container — where Claude Code
      runs. The [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#ccyolo\ccyolo]8;;\[0m bottle includes SSH, node, and the Claude CLI.

      This command is different from the sentry kludge. It builds the
      bottle AND drives the [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Hallmark\Hallmark]8;;\[0m into the nameplate
      automatically:

         [36mtt/rbw-cKB.KludgeBottle.sh[0m[36m ccyolo[0m

      Two kludge commands, two different jobs:
        [36mrbw-fk[0m  — standalone vessel kludge. Produces a hallmark, touches nothing else.
        [36mrbw-cKB[0m — nameplate-aware bottle kludge. Builds AND installs the hallmark.

      The sentry is shared across nameplates, so you kludge it standalone
      and drive the hallmark yourself. The bottle belongs to one nameplate,
      so the tool does the driving for you.

      [36m [*] [0mKludge-tagged bottle image found locally

[1;37mStep 2 — Drive sentry hallmark into the nameplate[0m

   A [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Nameplate\Nameplate]8;;\[0m is the file that defines a Crucible — it specifies
   what [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Hallmark\Hallmark]8;;\[0m to use for [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Sentry\Sentry]8;;\[0m, [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Pentacle\Pentacle]8;;\[0m, and
   [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bottle\Bottle]8;;\[0m containers. It lives at:

   [36m   .rbk/ccyolo/rbrn.env[0m

   The [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#RBRN\RBRN]8;;\[0m file has two hallmark fields — one for the sentry
   and one for the bottle. The bottle kludge (Step 1.2) already drove
   its hallmark. Now you drive the sentry hallmark by hand.

   Open the nameplate file and paste the sentry hallmark from Step 1.1
   into the [36mRBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK=[0m field.

   This is deliberately manual — it teaches you what [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Hallmark\Hallmarks]8;;\[0m are and
   where they live. You just saw the automated version with the [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bottle\Bottle]8;;\[0m;
   now you see the mechanism underneath.

    [*] [36mRBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK[0m = k260413155458-f9dcb6d9
    [*] [36mRBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK[0m = k260414083313-ce8b8648

   After editing, commit the change — the next [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Kludge\Kludge]8;;\[0m will need a
   clean tree:

   [36m   git add .rbk/ccyolo/rbrn.env[0m
   [36m   git commit -m "Drive sentry hallmark into ccyolo nameplate"[0m

[1;37mStep 3 — Charge the crucible[0m

   [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Charge\Charge]8;;\[0m starts three containers from your nameplate:

     [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Sentry\Sentry]8;;\[0m   — runs iptables + dnsmasq, enforces the network allowlist
     [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Pentacle\Pentacle]8;;\[0m  — establishes the network namespace shared with the bottle
     [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bottle\Bottle]8;;\[0m    — your workload container (Claude Code lives here)

   The sentry mediates all traffic. The bottle never touches the
   network directly — everything routes through the sentry's rules.

      [36mtt/rbw-cC.Charge.ccyolo.sh[0m

   Charge takes 10-30 seconds. It pulls the images from your local
   Docker, creates the enclave network, starts the containers, waits
   for the sentry to confirm its iptables rules are applied, then
   starts the bottle.

   [36m [*] [0mCrucible ccyolo is charged (bottle container running)

[1;37mStep 4 — Enter the container and run Claude Code[0m

   SSH into the bottle:

      [36mtt/rbw-cS.SshTo.ccyolo.sh[0m

   You land as the claude user in ~/workspace, which contains
   a small sample project. Run Claude Code:

   [36m   claude[0m

   Claude Code will prompt you to authenticate. It opens a URL —
   copy it to your workstation browser, sign in with your Claude
   subscription, and paste the code back into the terminal.

   [1;33mThe ccyolo bottle pins Claude Code to a specific version.[0m
   Versions after v2.1.89 have a regression where paste does not
   work in the OAuth input prompt through SSH or docker exec
   (github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/47745). If you
   update the pin and paste stops working, type the code manually.

   Once authenticated, Claude Code starts in full autonomy mode —
   no permission prompts. Inside a network-contained crucible,
   this is the correct posture: the sentry enforces the real
   security boundary, not the tool permission system.

   Try your first interaction:

   [36m   The count_words.sh script has bugs — can you find and fix them?[0m

   Watch Claude read the files, identify issues, and edit the code.
   The workspace persists across charge/quench cycles — your changes
   survive restarts.

   Why SSH instead of docker exec?

   Docker exec is laggy and breaks terminal resize — Claude Code's
   interactive display needs correct dimensions. SSH gives a proper
   login session with full terminal negotiation.

   If SSH fails, [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Rack\Rack]8;;\[0m is the diagnostic fallback —
   docker exec into the bottle to inspect state:

      [36mtt/rbw-cr.Rack.sh[0m[36m ccyolo[0m

[1;37mStep 5 — Verify network containment[0m

   From inside the [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bottle\Bottle]8;;\[0m (while SSH'd in), you can test what's reachable.
   The [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#ccyolo\ccyolo]8;;\[0m nameplate allows Anthropic and example.com (a test
   target). Everything else is blocked.

   Run these curl commands inside the bottle:

   [36m   curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' https://api.anthropic.com[0m
      Expected: 404 (API wants auth, not bare GET)

   [36m   curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' https://claude.ai[0m
      Expected: 403 (web app)

   [36m   curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' https://example.com[0m
      Expected: 200 (test target on allowlist)

   [36m   curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' --max-time 5 https://google.com[0m
      Expected: 000 or timeout (blocked)

   [36m   curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' --max-time 5 https://registry.npmjs.org[0m
      Expected: 000 or timeout (blocked)

   The [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Sentry\Sentry]8;;\[0m enforces this with two layers:
   dnsmasq resolves only whitelisted domains; iptables drops
   packets to any IP not in the CIDR allowlist. Both layers
   must agree for traffic to pass.

   example.com is included in the [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#ccyolo\ccyolo]8;;\[0m [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Nameplate\Nameplate]8;;\[0m specifically
   for this verification step — it proves the allowlist works
   for a non-Anthropic domain.

[1;37mStep 6 — Quench the crucible[0m

   [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Quench\Quench]8;;\[0m stops and removes all three containers and the
   enclave network:

      [36mtt/rbw-cQ.Quench.ccyolo.sh[0m

   Clean shutdown. The images stay cached locally — your next charge
   reuses them instantly.

[1;37mThe iteration loop[0m

You now have the full local cycle:

  1. Edit the Dockerfile or entrypoint
  2. Commit your changes ([34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Kludge\Kludge]8;;\[0m needs a clean tree)
  3. [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Kludge\Kludge]8;;\[0m the [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bottle\Bottle]8;;\[0m:
       [36mtt/rbw-cKB.KludgeBottle.sh[0m[36m ccyolo[0m
  4. Commit the [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Hallmark\Hallmark]8;;\[0m change
  5. [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Charge\Charge]8;;\[0m: Stop and restart
       [36mtt/rbw-cC.Charge.ccyolo.sh[0m
  6. SSH in:
       [36mtt/rbw-cS.SshTo.ccyolo.sh[0m
  7. Test your changes

[34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Charge\Charge]8;;\[0m tears down any prior state before starting, so you
don't need to Quench between iterations — just Charge again.

Steps 3-7 take under a minute. The [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Sentry\Sentry]8;;\[0m rarely changes, so
you almost never re-kludge it — the [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bottle\Bottle]8;;\[0m is your iteration
target.

Return to start: [36mtt/rbw-o.ONBOARDING.sh[0m
[rerun: b7]

   registry, no credentials. The fastest path from Dockerfile to running
   container without cloud image backup.

   [1;37mStep 1.1 — Kludge the sentry[0m

      The [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Sentry\Sentry]8;;\[0m is the gatekeeper container. It runs iptables
      and dnsmasq to enforce network policy — only domains you whitelist
      are reachable from inside.

      Kludge it:

         [36mtt/rbw-fk.LocalKludge.sh[0m[36m rbev-sentry-deb-tether[0m

      The output prints a hallmark tag — something like
      [36mk260413155458-f9dcb6d9[0m
      Copy it. You will paste it into the nameplate in Step 2.

       [ ] No kludge-tagged sentry image found

      [1;33mKludge requires a clean git tree.[0m
      If you have uncommitted changes, commit them first.  This is by
      design — container images not backed by git commits easy to confuse.

   [1;37mStep 1.2 — Kludge the bottle[0m

      The [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bottle\Bottle]8;;\[0m is your workload container — where Claude Code
      runs. The [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#ccyolo\ccyolo]8;;\[0m bottle includes SSH, node, and the Claude CLI.

      This command is different from the sentry kludge. It builds the
      bottle AND drives the [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Hallmark\Hallmark]8;;\[0m into the nameplate
      automatically:

         [36mtt/rbw-cKB.KludgeBottle.sh[0m[36m ccyolo[0m

      Two kludge commands, two different jobs:
        [36mrbw-fk[0m  — standalone vessel kludge. Produces a hallmark, touches nothing else.
        [36mrbw-cKB[0m — nameplate-aware bottle kludge. Builds AND installs the hallmark.

      The sentry is shared across nameplates, so you kludge it standalone
      and drive the hallmark yourself. The bottle belongs to one nameplate,
      so the tool does the driving for you.

      [36m [*] [0mKludge-tagged bottle image found locally

[1;37mStep 2 — Drive sentry hallmark into the nameplate[0m

   A [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Nameplate\Nameplate]8;;\[0m is the file that defines a Crucible — it specifies
   what [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Hallmark\Hallmark]8;;\[0m to use for [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Sentry\Sentry]8;;\[0m, [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Pentacle\Pentacle]8;;\[0m, and
   [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bottle\Bottle]8;;\[0m containers. It lives at:

   [36m   .rbk/ccyolo/rbrn.env[0m

   The [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#RBRN\RBRN]8;;\[0m file has two hallmark fields — one for the sentry
   and one for the bottle. The bottle kludge (Step 1.2) already drove
   its hallmark. Now you drive the sentry hallmark by hand.

   Open the nameplate file and paste the sentry hallmark from Step 1.1
   into the [36mRBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK=[0m field.

   This is deliberately manual — it teaches you what [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Hallmark\Hallmarks]8;;\[0m are and
   where they live. You just saw the automated version with the [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bottle\Bottle]8;;\[0m;
   now you see the mechanism underneath.

    [*] [36mRBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK[0m = k260413155458-f9dcb6d9
    [*] [36mRBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK[0m = k260414083313-ce8b8648

   After editing, commit the change — the next [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Kludge\Kludge]8;;\[0m will need a
   clean tree:

   [36m   git add .rbk/ccyolo/rbrn.env[0m
   [36m   git commit -m "Drive sentry hallmark into ccyolo nameplate"[0m

[1;37mStep 3 — Charge the crucible[0m

   [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Charge\Charge]8;;\[0m starts three containers from your nameplate:

     [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Sentry\Sentry]8;;\[0m   — runs iptables + dnsmasq, enforces the network allowlist
     [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Pentacle\Pentacle]8;;\[0m  — establishes the network namespace shared with the bottle
     [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bottle\Bottle]8;;\[0m    — your workload container (Claude Code lives here)

   The sentry mediates all traffic. The bottle never touches the
   network directly — everything routes through the sentry's rules.

      [36mtt/rbw-cC.Charge.ccyolo.sh[0m

   Charge takes 10-30 seconds. It pulls the images from your local
   Docker, creates the enclave network, starts the containers, waits
   for the sentry to confirm its iptables rules are applied, then
   starts the bottle.

   [36m [*] [0mCrucible ccyolo is charged (bottle container running)

[1;37mStep 4 — Enter the container and run Claude Code[0m

   SSH into the bottle:

      [36mtt/rbw-cS.SshTo.ccyolo.sh[0m

   You land as the claude user in ~/workspace, which contains
   a small sample project. Run Claude Code:

   [36m   claude[0m

   Claude Code will prompt you to authenticate. It opens a URL —
   copy it to your workstation browser, sign in with your Claude
   subscription, and paste the code back into the terminal.

   [1;33mThe ccyolo bottle pins Claude Code to a specific version.[0m
   Versions after v2.1.89 have a regression where paste does not
   work in the OAuth input prompt through SSH or docker exec
   (github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/47745). If you
   update the pin and paste stops working, type the code manually.

   Once authenticated, Claude Code starts in full autonomy mode —
   no permission prompts. Inside a network-contained crucible,
   this is the correct posture: the sentry enforces the real
   security boundary, not the tool permission system.

   Try your first interaction:

   [36m   The count_words.sh script has bugs — can you find and fix them?[0m

   Watch Claude read the files, identify issues, and edit the code.
   The workspace persists across charge/quench cycles — your changes
   survive restarts.

   Why SSH instead of docker exec?

   Docker exec is laggy and breaks terminal resize — Claude Code's
   interactive display needs correct dimensions. SSH gives a proper
   login session with full terminal negotiation.

   If SSH fails, [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Rack\Rack]8;;\[0m is the diagnostic fallback —
   docker exec into the bottle to inspect state:

      [36mtt/rbw-cr.Rack.sh[0m[36m ccyolo[0m

[1;37mStep 5 — Verify network containment[0m

   From inside the [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bottle\Bottle]8;;\[0m (while SSH'd in), you can test what's reachable.
   The [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#ccyolo\ccyolo]8;;\[0m nameplate allows Anthropic and example.com (a test
   target). Everything else is blocked.

   Run these curl commands inside the bottle:

   [36m   curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' https://api.anthropic.com[0m
      Expected: 404 (API wants auth, not bare GET)

   [36m   curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' https://claude.ai[0m
      Expected: 403 (web app)

   [36m   curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' https://example.com[0m
      Expected: 200 (test target on allowlist)

   [36m   curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' --max-time 5 https://google.com[0m
      Expected: 000 or timeout (blocked)

   [36m   curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' --max-time 5 https://registry.npmjs.org[0m
      Expected: 000 or timeout (blocked)

   The [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Sentry\Sentry]8;;\[0m enforces this with two layers:
   dnsmasq resolves only whitelisted domains; iptables drops
   packets to any IP not in the CIDR allowlist. Both layers
   must agree for traffic to pass.

   example.com is included in the [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#ccyolo\ccyolo]8;;\[0m [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Nameplate\Nameplate]8;;\[0m specifically
   for this verification step — it proves the allowlist works
   for a non-Anthropic domain.

[1;37mStep 6 — Quench the crucible[0m

   [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Quench\Quench]8;;\[0m stops and removes all three containers and the
   enclave network:

      [36mtt/rbw-cQ.Quench.ccyolo.sh[0m

   Clean shutdown. The images stay cached locally — your next charge
   reuses them instantly.

[1;37mThe iteration loop[0m

You now have the full local cycle:

  1. Edit the Dockerfile or entrypoint
  2. Commit your changes ([34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Kludge\Kludge]8;;\[0m needs a clean tree)
  3. [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Kludge\Kludge]8;;\[0m the [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bottle\Bottle]8;;\[0m:
       [36mtt/rbw-cKB.KludgeBottle.sh[0m[36m ccyolo[0m
  4. Commit the [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Hallmark\Hallmark]8;;\[0m change
  5. [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Charge\Charge]8;;\[0m: Stop and restart
       [36mtt/rbw-cC.Charge.ccyolo.sh[0m
  6. SSH in:
       [36mtt/rbw-cS.SshTo.ccyolo.sh[0m
  7. Test your changes

[34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Charge\Charge]8;;\[0m tears down any prior state before starting, so you
don't need to Quench between iterations — just Charge again.

Steps 3-7 take under a minute. The [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Sentry\Sentry]8;;\[0m rarely changes, so
you almost never re-kludge it — the [34;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bottle\Bottle]8;;\[0m is your iteration
target.

Return to start: [36mtt/rbw-o.ONBOARDING.sh[0m

content.text:
All seven tasks complete. Summary of what was built:

**New files:**
- `Tools/buk/buy_yelp.sh` — four capture functions (`buy_link_capture`, `buy_cmd_capture`, `buy_ui_capture`, `buy_tt_capture`), three configurators (dispatch/unconditional/plain), sentinel/kindle pattern
- `Tools/rbk/rbyc_common.sh` — 46 base constants + 10 variant forms from the 38 paddock anchors plus additional handbook anchors

**Modified files:**
- `Tools/buk/buh_handbook.sh` — added `buh_line` with `%b`→`%s` mapping (yelps are pre-rendered; `%b` double-interprets OSC-8 ST sequences)
- `Tools/rbk/rbho_cli.sh` — sources `buy_yelp.sh` and `rbyc_common.sh`, kindles `rbyc` after `rbrr`
- `Tools/rbk/rbho_onboarding.sh` — converted `rbho_first_crucible()` from combinator style to yelp+constants
- `CLAUDE.md` — added BUY and RBYC acronym entries

**Discovery during implementation:** `printf %b` cannot safely pass pre-rendered yelp fragments because the OSC-8 String Terminator (`ESC \`) followed by certain content chars (like `\c` = cease output) causes truncation. The `buh_line` implementation maps `%b` to `%s` internally, preserving the docket's calling convention while avoiding the double-interpretation.

Ready to notch when you are — aware another chat is working, so I'll wait for your signal.

content.text: Notched as `3a547e38`.

message.content:
I did observe a potential simplification: I saw that in several places you changed to...
      1125 +  buh_line "Steps 3-7 take under a minute. The %b rarely changes, so" "${RBYC_SENTRY}"
...instead of...
      1125 +  buh_line "Steps 3-7 take under a minute. The %b rarely changes, so ${RBYC_SENTRY}"
...which is visually a bit simpler.  Thoughts?

content.text:
That works — and it's genuinely simpler. The yelp fragment contains literal bytes (not `\033` strings), so bash interpolation puts them directly into the format string. Printf passes literal ESC bytes through unchanged. No `%b`/`%s` dance needed for those lines.

One thing to know: if a yelp fragment ever contained a literal `%` (e.g., a percent-encoded URL like `%20`), printf would try to interpret it as a format specifier when it's in the format string. That can't happen with the current vocabulary constants (anchors are simple words, URLs are clean), but it could bite an inline `buy_cmd_capture` of user-facing text containing `%`.

A clean convention: direct interpolation for `RBYC_*` constants (known-safe content), `%b` argument for ad-hoc `buy_*_capture` results where the content is less controlled. That gives you the visual simplicity on the lines that appear most often without losing safety.

Want me to go through `rbho_first_crucible()` and apply that simplification where it applies?

message.content: I'm not quite following.  Do we have a good name for the set of buy_*_capture functions?  Might even deserve a quoin in BUS0...  I wonder if we can just forbid them from trying to handle %?

content.text:
The collective name already exists — the docket calls them **yelps**. A yelp is a pre-rendered formatted string fragment produced by a `buy_*` capture function. The capture functions are the producers; the yelp is the artifact.

And yes — forbidding `%` is the right move. It's a premise: **yelp content must not contain literal `%` characters.** That's a genuine design constraint, not an artificial one. Look at what flows through capture functions:

- `buy_link_capture` — anchor names (`"Depot"`, `"Hallmark"`) and README URLs. No `%`.
- `buy_cmd_capture` — colophon names (`"rbw-fk"`, `"rbw-cKB"`). No `%`.
- `buy_ui_capture` — UI element names. No `%`.
- `buy_tt_capture` — tabtarget paths. No `%`.

The constraint matches reality. And it simplifies everything: if yelps never contain `%`, then direct interpolation is always safe, and `buh_line` collapses to:

```bash
buh_line() {
  zbuh_sentinel
  printf '%s\n' "${z_buh_body_indent}${1}" >&2
}
```

No format string, no `%b`/`%s` mapping, no argument list. Callers just write `buh_line "A ${RBYC_VESSEL} is a spec..."`. 

That's load-bearing simplification — the premise earns its existence by eliminating an entire category of complexity (format-string escaping, double-interpretation bugs, the `%b`→`%s` workaround).

It does deserve a quoin in BUS0. Want me to make both changes — simplify `buh_line` + mint the quoin?

message.content: please.

content.content:
529	=== Fact-File Pattern
530	
531	[[busff_fact_file]]
532	//axl_voices axo_entity
533	{busff_fact_file}::
534	Inter-step communication pattern using named output files.
535	+
536	A producer writes a value to `${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/${CONSTANT}` during command execution.
537	A consumer reads it from `${ZBUTO_BURV_OUTPUT}/current/${CONSTANT}` after invocation.
538	The kindle constant name is the contract between producer and consumer.
539	+
540	Example:
541	+
542	[source,bash]
543	----
544	# Producer (in command implementation):
545	echo "${z_hallmark}" > "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/${RBF_FACT_HALLMARK}"
546	
547	# Consumer (in test case, after buto_tt_expect_ok):
548	z_hallmark=$(<"${ZBUTO_BURV_OUTPUT}/current/${RBF_FACT_HALLMARK}")
549	----
550	+
551	The `current/` path segment maps
552	{burd_output_dir}
553	inside the BURV isolation tree.
554	Fact-file constants are defined as `readonly` in kindle (e.g., `RBF_FACT_HALLMARK`).
555	
556	[[buf_write_fact]]
557	//axl_voices axo_entity
558	{buf_write_fact}::
559	Dual-write fact-file primitive.
560	Writes a named file to both
561	{burd_output_dir}
562	and
563	{burd_temp_dir}.
564	Dies if either copy already exists — a preexisting file indicates a double-write bug.
565	+
566	{burd_output_dir}
567	is the latest-command convenience copy, cleared on next dispatch.
568	{burd_temp_dir}
569	is the durable copy, addressable by anyone who captured the path.
570	+
571	All fact-file producers must use
572	{buf_write_fact}
573	to ensure durable copies exist.
574	+
575	[source,bash]
576	----
577	# Single-value fact:
578	buf_write_fact "${RBF_FACT_HALLMARK}" "${z_hallmark}"
579	
580	# Multi-field fact (e.g., burx.env):
581	buf_write_fact "${BUF_burx_env}" "${z_content}"
582	----
583	
584	[[buf_burx_env]]
585	//axpof_fact
586	{buf_burx_env}::
587	Tinder constant naming the
588	{burx_regime}
589	fact-file (`burx.env`).
590	Written by bud_dispatch.sh via
591	{buf_write_fact}
592	at dispatch start, with terminal fields appended at completion.
593	
594	== Regime Configuration
595	
596	BUK defines two
597	{axrg_regime_s}
598	for project and developer configuration.

file.content:
=== Fact-File Pattern

[[busff_fact_file]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busff_fact_file}::
Inter-step communication pattern using named output files.
+
A producer writes a value to `${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/${CONSTANT}` during command execution.
A consumer reads it from `${ZBUTO_BURV_OUTPUT}/current/${CONSTANT}` after invocation.
The kindle constant name is the contract between producer and consumer.
+
Example:
+
[source,bash]
----
# Producer (in command implementation):
echo "${z_hallmark}" > "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/${RBF_FACT_HALLMARK}"

# Consumer (in test case, after buto_tt_expect_ok):
z_hallmark=$(<"${ZBUTO_BURV_OUTPUT}/current/${RBF_FACT_HALLMARK}")
----
+
The `current/` path segment maps
{burd_output_dir}
inside the BURV isolation tree.
Fact-file constants are defined as `readonly` in kindle (e.g., `RBF_FACT_HALLMARK`).

[[buf_write_fact]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{buf_write_fact}::
Dual-write fact-file primitive.
Writes a named file to both
{burd_output_dir}
and
{burd_temp_dir}.
Dies if either copy already exists — a preexisting file indicates a double-write bug.
+
{burd_output_dir}
is the latest-command convenience copy, cleared on next dispatch.
{burd_temp_dir}
is the durable copy, addressable by anyone who captured the path.
+
All fact-file producers must use
{buf_write_fact}
to ensure durable copies exist.
+
[source,bash]
----
# Single-value fact:
buf_write_fact "${RBF_FACT_HALLMARK}" "${z_hallmark}"

# Multi-field fact (e.g., burx.env):
buf_write_fact "${BUF_burx_env}" "${z_content}"
----

[[buf_burx_env]]
//axpof_fact
{buf_burx_env}::
Tinder constant naming the
{burx_regime}
fact-file (`burx.env`).
Written by bud_dispatch.sh via
{buf_write_fact}
at dispatch start, with terminal fields appended at completion.

== Regime Configuration

BUK defines two
{axrg_regime_s}
for project and developer configuration.

== Overview

The Bash Utilities Specification defines the tabtarget dispatch system — the infrastructure for tab-completion-friendly launchers that route to kit formularies.

BUK invented tabtargets; BUK defines them.

For universal naming vocabulary (Cipher, Signet, Epithet, Inscription, Vesture), see VLS-VoxLiturgicalSpec.adoc.

== Tabtarget Vesture

[[bust_tabtarget]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{bust_tabtarget}::
Vesture for tabtarget launcher identifiers.
Defines how launcher scripts in `tt/` are named.
+
* signet_case: lowercase with hyphen (the colophon)
* separator: `.`
* epithet_case: PascalCase (the frontispiece)
* envelope: `.sh`
+
Pattern: `{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh`
+
Examples:
+
* `rbw-cr.Rack.sh` — RBW workbench, Rack operation
* `rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh` — same, with imprint specifying target
* `jjw-.HeatMount.sh` — JJK workbench, HeatMount operation
* `buw-tt.LauncherList.sh` — BUK tabtarget testbench, LauncherList operation

== Dispatch

The dispatch vocabulary defines the tabtarget launcher system.
A
{busdl_launcher}
validates arguments and delegates to a
{busdm_formulary}.
The
{busdc_colophon}
identifies the routing path;
the
{busdf_frontispiece}
provides human-readable description;
the
{busdi_imprint}
embeds target parameters.

[[busdc_colophon]]
//axl_voices axt_string
{busdc_colophon}::
The routing identifier in a tabtarget inscription.
A
{busdc_colophon}
determines which
{busdm_formulary}
handles the dispatch.
Includes the hyphen as part of its identity (e.g., `rbw-`, `buw-tt-`).
+
The
{busdc_colophon}
is the first component of the tabtarget pattern, before the first dot separator.
+
Examples:
+
* `rbw-` routes to RBW workbench
* `jjw-` routes to JJK workbench
* `buw-tt-` routes to BUK tabtarget testbench

[[busdf_frontispiece]]
//axl_voices axt_string
{busdf_frontispiece}::
The human-readable description in a tabtarget inscription.
A
{busdf_frontispiece}
uses PascalCase and describes the operation.
+
Appears after the
{busdc_colophon}
and dot separator.
+
Examples: `Build`, `Rack`, `RunVVX`, `HeatMount`.

[[busdi_imprint]]
//axl_voices axt_string
{busdi_imprint}::
An optional embedded parameter in a tabtarget inscription.
An
{busdi_imprint}
specifies a target, instance, or configuration variant.
+
Appears after a second dot separator following the
{busdf_frontispiece}.
+
Examples:
+
* `tadmor` in `rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh`
* `srjcl` in `rbw-cr.Rack.srjcl.sh`

[[busdm_formulary]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busdm_formulary}::
A component that routes
{busdc_colophon_s}
to implementations.
Each
{busdm_formulary}
registers handlers for a set of
{busdc_colophon_s}
and dispatches to the appropriate function.
+
Types:
+
* **Workbench** (`{prefix}w_workbench.sh`): Routes commands for a kit
* **Testbench** (`{prefix}t_testbench.sh`): Routes tests for a kit
+
A
{busdm_formulary}
is sourced by the
{busdl_launcher}
and provides the dispatch function.

[[busdl_launcher]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busdl_launcher}::
A bootstrap script in the `tt/` directory that validates arguments and delegates to a
{busdm_formulary}.
A
{busdl_launcher}
is the entry point for tabtarget invocation.
+
Naming follows
{bust_tabtarget}:
`{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh`
+
Responsibilities:
+
* Parse
{busdc_colophon}
from own filename
* Validate required arguments
* Source the target
{busdm_formulary}
* Invoke the dispatched function

== Routing Behavior

=== Dispatch Flow

. User invokes launcher: `./tt/rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh`
. Launcher parses its filename to extract:
* {busdc_colophon}:
`rbw-cr`
* {busdf_frontispiece}:
`Rack`
* {busdi_imprint}:
`tadmor`
. Launcher sources the appropriate
{busdm_formulary}
(`rbw_workbench.sh`)
. Formulary's dispatch function routes based on frontispiece
. Target function executes with imprint as parameter

=== Workbench Pattern

[source,bash]
----
# Tools/{kit}/{prefix}w_workbench.sh

{prefix}w_dispatch() {
    local frontispiece="$1"
    local imprint="$2"
    shift 2

    case "$frontispiece" in
        Build)          {prefix}w_build "$imprint" "$@" ;;
        Rack)           {prefix}w_rack "$imprint" "$@" ;;
        *)              buc_fatal "Unknown frontispiece: $frontispiece" ;;
    esac
}
----

=== Testbench Pattern

Testbenches follow the same pattern but route to test functions:

[source,bash]
----
# Tools/{kit}/{prefix}t_testbench.sh

{prefix}t_dispatch() {
    local frontispiece="$1"
    shift

    case "$frontispiece" in
        Unit)        {prefix}t_unit "$@" ;;
        Integration) {prefix}t_integration "$@" ;;
        *)           buc_fatal "Unknown test: $frontispiece" ;;
    esac
}
----

=== Test Registry Model

The test registry (`butr_registry.sh`) uses two orthogonal enrollment dimensions:

**Fixture** — init/setup execution context.
Each test case belongs to exactly one fixture.
Fixtures provide isolation via `_tsuite` subshell boundaries.
Registered with `butr_fixture_enroll(name, init_fn, setup_fn)`.

**Suite** — cross-cutting selection group (N:M with cases).
A case can belong to multiple suites.
Suites classify by dependency tier (what environment must exist), not by test methodology.
A suite is a membership tag.
Suites enable suite dispatch: run a cross-cutting subset of cases grouped by their owning fixtures.
Suite context is set with `butr_suite_enroll(SUITE1 [SUITE2...])` before case enrollments.
Queried by `butd_run_suite(suite_name)`.

Standard suite constants (defined as `readonly` in testbench kindle):

* `BUTR_SUITE_FAST` — no external dependencies, CI-safe
* `BUTR_SUITE_SERVICE` — needs credentials, no container runtime
* `BUTR_SUITE_COMPLETE` — full test matrix including container runtime

Dispatch entry points:

* `butd_run_all()` — all fixtures, all cases
* `butd_run_fixture(name [case...])` — single fixture, optional case filter
* `butd_run_one(fn)` — single case with its fixture setup
* `butd_run_suite(suite)` — cross-cutting suite, grouped by fixture

=== Colophon Conventions

{busdc_colophon_s}
follow a structure that encodes both kit identity and formulary type:

* `{cipher}-` — default workbench (e.g., `rbw-`, `jjw-`)
* `{cipher}-t-` — testbench (e.g., `buw-t-`, `jjw-t-`)
* `{cipher}-{variant}-` — specialized workbench (e.g., `rbw-cr-` for crucible rack operations)

The trailing hyphen is always part of the colophon — it separates the routing prefix from the dot-delimited tabtarget components.

== Test Invocation

The test invocation vocabulary defines how test cases exercise code under controlled capture.
All invocation tiers share a common primitive,
`zbuto_invoke`,
which captures stdout, stderr, exit status, and optional BURV output into
{busig_stdout},
{busig_stderr},
{busig_status},
and
{busig_burv_output}.

=== Invocation Tiers

Three tiers provide increasing levels of resolution:

[[busit_unit]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busit_unit}::
Invokes a raw function or command directly via `zbuto_invoke`.
No tabtarget file or workbench routing involved.
+
API: `buto_unit_expect_ok(command...)`, `buto_unit_expect_fatal(command...)`, `buto_unit_expect_ok_stdout(expected, command...)`

[[busit_tabtarget]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busit_tabtarget}::
Resolves a
{busdc_colophon}
to a
{busdl_launcher}
file in `tt/`, then invokes it via `zbuto_invoke`.
Dies if no tabtarget matches or if multiple matches exist.
+
API: `buto_tt_expect_ok(colophon, args...)`, `buto_tt_expect_fatal(colophon, args...)`

[[busit_launch]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busit_launch}::
Invokes a workbench dispatch function directly, bypassing the
{busdl_launcher}
file.
Useful when exercising workbench routing without a tabtarget on disk.
+
API: `buto_launch_expect_ok(launcher, colophon, args...)`, `buto_launch_expect_fatal(launcher, colophon, args...)`

=== Capture Globals

Every call to `zbuto_invoke` sets four globals that subsequent assertions can inspect.

[[busig_stdout]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{busig_stdout}::
Captured stdout from the invoked command.

[[busig_stderr]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{busig_stderr}::
Captured stderr from the invoked command.

[[busig_status]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_integer
{busig_status}::
Exit code from the invoked command.

[[busig_burv_output]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{busig_burv_output}::
BURV output directory for the invocation.
Empty when
{busx_burv_root}
is not set.
When set, contains the invocation's isolated output tree rooted at
`{BUTE_BURV_ROOT}/invoke-NNNNN/output`.

=== Case Boundary

[[busx_tcase]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busx_tcase}::
The case isolation runner (`zbute_tcase`).
Executes a single test case function in a subshell with per-case temporary state.
Sets
{busx_temp_dir}
and
{busx_burv_root}
before invoking the case function.
+
The subshell boundary ensures that variable mutations, function redefinitions, and side effects in one case cannot leak to another.

[[busx_temp_dir]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{busx_temp_dir}::
Per-case temporary directory (`BUT_TEMP_DIR`), set by
{busx_tcase}.
Created as a subdirectory of the fixture's root temp dir, named after the case function.

[[busx_burv_root]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{busx_burv_root}::
Per-case BURV isolation root (`BUTE_BURV_ROOT`), set by
{busx_tcase}.
When present, all invocation tiers create per-invoke BURV directories under this root, enabling multi-step test sequences to produce and consume isolated output.

=== Fact-File Pattern

[[busff_fact_file]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{busff_fact_file}::
Inter-step communication pattern using named output files.
+
A producer writes a value to `${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/${CONSTANT}` during command execution.
A consumer reads it from `${ZBUTO_BURV_OUTPUT}/current/${CONSTANT}` after invocation.
The kindle constant name is the contract between producer and consumer.
+
Example:
+
[source,bash]
----
# Producer (in command implementation):
echo "${z_hallmark}" > "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/${RBF_FACT_HALLMARK}"

# Consumer (in test case, after buto_tt_expect_ok):
z_hallmark=$(<"${ZBUTO_BURV_OUTPUT}/current/${RBF_FACT_HALLMARK}")
----
+
The `current/` path segment maps
{burd_output_dir}
inside the BURV isolation tree.
Fact-file constants are defined as `readonly` in kindle (e.g., `RBF_FACT_HALLMARK`).

[[buf_write_fact]]
//axl_voices axo_entity
{buf_write_fact}::
Dual-write fact-file primitive.
Writes a named file to both
{burd_output_dir}
and
{burd_temp_dir}.
Dies if either copy already exists — a preexisting file indicates a double-write bug.
+
{burd_output_dir}
is the latest-command convenience copy, cleared on next dispatch.
{burd_temp_dir}
is the durable copy, addressable by anyone who captured the path.
+
All fact-file producers must use
{buf_write_fact}
to ensure durable copies exist.
+
[source,bash]
----
# Single-value fact:
buf_write_fact "${RBF_FACT_HALLMARK}" "${z_hallmark}"

# Multi-field fact (e.g., burx.env):
buf_write_fact "${BUF_burx_env}" "${z_content}"
----

[[buf_burx_env]]
//axpof_fact
{buf_burx_env}::
Tinder constant naming the
{burx_regime}
fact-file (`burx.env`).
Written by bud_dispatch.sh via
{buf_write_fact}
at dispatch start, with terminal fields appended at completion.

== Regime Configuration

BUK defines two
{axrg_regime_s}
for project and developer configuration.
{axrg_assignment_s}
use
{axf_bash}
format (shell variable assignment files that are sourced).

=== Regime Prefixes

[[burc_prefix]]
//axl_voices axrg_prefix
{burc_prefix}::
Bash Utility Configuration Regime

[[burs_prefix]]
//axl_voices axrg_prefix
{burs_prefix}::
Bash Utility Station Regime

[[burh_prefix]]
//axl_voices axrg_prefix
{burh_prefix}::
Bash Utility Host Regime

[[bure_prefix]]
//axl_voices axrg_prefix
{bure_prefix}::
Bash Utility Environment Regime

=== Kit Regime Operations

[[bukro_kindle]]
//axvr_kindle
{bukro_kindle}::
Silent initialization of Bash Utility Kit regime state from
{axrg_assignment_s}.
Sets defaults, detects unexpected variables, builds derived structures.

[[bukro_validate]]
//axvr_validate
{bukro_validate}::
Strict field validation for Bash Utility Kit
{axrg_regime_s}.
Checks all type and constraint requirements via buv_env_* validators.

[[bukro_render]]
//axvr_render
{bukro_render}::
Diagnostic display of Bash Utility Kit
{axrg_regime}
state.

=== BURC Regime

[[burc_regime]]
//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_file_sourced axrd_singleton
{burc_regime}::
Configuration regime for project-level BUK settings.
BURC assignment files in `.buk/burc.env` define repository structure and tabtarget infrastructure.

[[burc_station_file]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_path
{burc_station_file}::
Path to the developer's personal
{burs_regime}
assignment file.

[[burc_tabtarget_dir]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_path
{burc_tabtarget_dir}::
Directory containing
{busdl_launcher}
scripts.

[[burc_tabtarget_delimiter]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
{burc_tabtarget_delimiter}::
Single-character token separator in
{bust_tabtarget}
inscriptions.

[[burc_tools_dir]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_path
{burc_tools_dir}::
Directory containing BUK utilities and project tool scripts.

[[burc_project_root]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_path
{burc_project_root}::
Path from assignment file location to the project root directory.

[[burc_managed_kits]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
{burc_managed_kits}::
Comma-separated list of kits managed by vvx.
At release time, determines which kits are built into the binary.
At install time, must exactly match the parcel's kit list.

[[burc_temp_root_dir]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_path
{burc_temp_root_dir}::
Root directory for BUK temporary files.

[[burc_output_root_dir]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_path
{burc_output_root_dir}::
Root directory for BUK command output files.

[[burc_log_last]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
{burc_log_last}::
Filename stem for the most recent command execution log.

[[burc_log_ext]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
{burc_log_ext}::
File extension for log files, without leading dot.

=== BURS Regime

[[burs_regime]]
//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_file_sourced axrd_singleton
{burs_regime}::
Configuration regime for developer-level BUK settings.
BURS assignment files are personal to each developer and not committed to version control.

[[burs_user]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
{burs_user}::
Local developer username.
Routes to the correct `.buk/users/` subdirectory for
{burh_regime}
profiles.

[[burs_log_dir]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_path
{burs_log_dir}::
Directory where this developer stores BUK operation logs.

=== BURH Regime

[[burh_regime]]
//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_file_sourced
{burh_regime}::
Per-user, per-connection-profile regime for SSH access to remote hosts.
Unlike singleton regimes, BURH is multi-instance: one assignment file per SSH connection,
stored at `.buk/users/{burs_user}/<alias>/burh.env`.
Each profile describes one SSH connection — one key, one alias, one entry point.

[[burh_host]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
{burh_host}::
IP address or hostname of the remote machine.

[[burh_user]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
{burh_user}::
Username on the remote host.
May differ from the local
{burs_user}.

[[burh_alias]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
{burh_alias}::
SSH alias and key filename.
Must match the directory name containing this profile's assignment file.

[[burh_ssh_pubkey]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
{burh_ssh_pubkey}::
Full SSH public key line (e.g., `ssh-ed25519 AAAA... user@host`).
Git-transported: no private key material.
Used by handbook procedures to render `authorized_keys` entries and `ssh-keygen` commands.

[[burh_key_file]]
//axvr_variable axd_optional axtu_string
{burh_key_file}::
SSH private key filename within `~/.ssh/`.
Empty defaults to {burh_alias} (one key per connection).
Localhost profiles set `id_ed25519` to share the invoking user's default key.

[[burh_command]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
{burh_command}::
Shell command for `command=` routing in SSH `authorized_keys`.
Empty for direct-shell connections (Linux, macOS, localhost — no routing needed).
When set, determines which shell or environment the connection launches
(e.g., `C:\cygwin64\bin\bash.exe -l` for Cygwin, `wsl.exe -d Ubuntu` for WSL).

=== BURD Regime

[[burd_prefix]]
//axl_voices axrg_prefix
{burd_prefix}::
Bash Utility Dispatch Runtime

[[burd_regime]]
//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_constructed axrd_singleton
{burd_regime}::
Ephemeral runtime state assembled by bud_dispatch.sh during tabtarget execution.
Unlike file-sourced regimes, BURD variables are constructed inline — no assignment file exists.
The dispatch main function IS the kindle: zbud_setup sources configuration and derives computed state, zbud_process_args parses the tabtarget into tokens.

==== Input Variables

[[burd_group_input]]
//axl_voices axhrgb_group
{burd_group_input}::
Variables set by the caller before dispatch invocation.

[[burd_regime_file]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burd_regime_file}::
Path to the BURC regime assignment file to source during setup.

[[burd_no_log]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_optional
{burd_no_log}::
When non-empty, suppresses all log file creation and output.

[[burd_interactive]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_optional
{burd_interactive}::
When non-empty, enables interactive mode with uncurated tee logging.

[[burd_coordinator_script]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path axd_required
{burd_coordinator_script}::
Path to the coordinator script (workbench/testbench) that handles the dispatched command.

[[burd_launcher]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path axd_required
{burd_launcher}::
Path to the launcher bootstrap script, set by the tabtarget before exec.

[[burd_station_file]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path axd_required
{burd_station_file}::
Absolute path to the developer's station regime assignment file.
Set by the launcher from BURC_STATION_FILE.

[[burd_term_cols]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_integer
{burd_term_cols}::
Terminal column width at dispatch time.
Detected from stty if available, defaults to 80.

==== Computed Variables

[[burd_group_computed]]
//axl_voices axhrgb_group
{burd_group_computed}::
Variables derived during dispatch setup.

[[burd_now_stamp]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burd_now_stamp}::
Unique timestamp combining date, time, PID, and random component.

[[burd_now_epoch]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_integer
{burd_now_epoch}::
UTC epoch seconds captured from same `date` invocation as {burd_now_stamp}.

[[burd_temp_dir]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burd_temp_dir}::
Ephemeral directory for this dispatch invocation.

[[burd_output_dir]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burd_output_dir}::
Fixed output directory cleared and recreated on each dispatch.

[[burd_transcript]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burd_transcript}::
Path to the transcript file within the temporary directory.

[[burd_git_context]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burd_git_context}::
Git describe output capturing repository state at dispatch time.

[[burd_log_last]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burd_log_last}::
Path to the "last" log file, overwritten each dispatch.

[[burd_log_same]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burd_log_same}::
Path to the curated "same" log file for the command+target combination.

[[burd_log_hist]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burd_log_hist}::
Path to the timestamped historical log file.

==== Parsed Variables

[[burd_group_parsed]]
//axl_voices axhrgb_group
{burd_group_parsed}::
Variables extracted from the tabtarget filename during argument processing.

[[burd_command]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burd_command}::
Primary command token (first token from tabtarget split).

[[burd_target]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burd_target}::
Complete tabtarget string as received by dispatch.

[[burd_cli_args]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_repeated
{burd_cli_args}::
Array of additional CLI arguments passed after the tabtarget.

[[burd_token_1]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burd_token_1}::
Positional token 1 from tabtarget split (the colophon).

[[burd_token_2]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burd_token_2}::
Positional token 2 from tabtarget split (the frontispiece).

[[burd_token_3]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burd_token_3}::
Positional token 3 from tabtarget split (the imprint, if present).

[[burd_token_4]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burd_token_4}::
Positional token 4 from tabtarget split.

[[burd_token_5]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burd_token_5}::
Positional token 5 from tabtarget split.

=== BURE Regime

[[bure_regime]]
//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_constructed axrd_singleton
{bure_regime}::
Ambient regime for caller-supplied behavioral overrides to BUK primitives.
Unlike file-sourced regimes, BURE variables are set directly in the caller's environment.
Kindled and validated when buc_command.sh is sourced.

[[bure_countdown]]
//axvr_variable axtu_string axd_optional
{bure_countdown}::
Countdown behavior override.
When set to `skip`, buc_countdown bypasses interactive countdown and tty output.
When unset, countdown proceeds normally with interactive tty cancel window.
Any other value fails validation.

[[bure_verbose]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_integer
{bure_verbose}::
Verbosity level controlling diagnostic output.
0=silent, 1=show messages, 2=enable bash trace.

[[bure_color]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{bure_color}::
Color output policy.
Input as auto/0/1; resolved to 0 or 1 during dispatch.
Respects NO_COLOR environment convention.

==== Tweak Mechanism

The tweak mechanism provides a single caller-supplied name/value override that flows through invocation without BUK interpreting its semantics.
BUK enrolls, validates, and propagates the pair; consuming kits (e.g., RBK) check the name and act on the value.

Constraint: one tweak per invocation.
The caller sets at most one
{bure_tweak_name}/{bure_tweak_value}
pair in the environment before invoking a tabtarget.
The pair is ambient — it propagates through `zbuto_invoke` like all BURE variables, without requiring re-kindle in the invoked command.

[[bure_tweak_name]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_optional
{bure_tweak_name}::
Consumer-interpreted tweak identifier.
The name selects which behavioral override the consuming kit applies.
BUK validates length (0–64 characters) but assigns no meaning to the value.
+
Example: `graft_image` — tells RBK to substitute the graft source image.

[[bure_tweak_value]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_optional
{bure_tweak_value}::
Consumer-interpreted tweak payload.
The value is opaque to BUK; its format and interpretation are defined by whatever kit checks
{bure_tweak_name}.
BUK validates length (0–256 characters) but assigns no meaning to the value.
+
Either variable may be set independently — BUK does not enforce pairing.
Consumer kits that require both must enforce that themselves.

==== Exchange Labels

[[bure_label]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_xname axd_optional
{bure_label}::
Correlation label for cross-project exchange.
When set, propagated verbatim into
{burx_label}
during dispatch.
When unset, BUD writes empty string.
Maximum 120 characters, xname format.
+
BUD writes whatever
{bure_label}
contains without interpreting the semantics.

=== BURX Regime

[[burx_prefix]]
//axl_voices axrg_prefix
{burx_prefix}::
Bash Utility Exchange Regime

[[burx_regime]]
//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_constructed axrd_singleton
{burx_regime}::
Exchange regime for cross-project dispatch state.
Written by bud_dispatch.sh to both
{burd_output_dir}
and
{burd_temp_dir}
as `burx.env` via
{buf_write_fact}.
Unlike single-project regimes,
{burx_regime}
is designed to be read across a remote project boundary.
+
Variables appear in two phases:
initial fields are written at dispatch start;
terminal fields are appended at dispatch completion.
Absence of
{burx_exit_status}
indicates the dispatch is still running.

[[burx_pid]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_integer
{burx_pid}::
Process ID of the dispatched command.

[[burx_began_at]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burx_began_at}::
Nanosecond-precision timestamp when dispatch started.
Format: `YYYYMMDD-HHMMSS.NNNNNNNNN`.

[[burx_tabtarget]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string
{burx_tabtarget}::
The tabtarget that was dispatched.
Corresponds to
{burd_target}.

[[burx_temp_dir]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burx_temp_dir}::
Absolute path to the dispatch temporary directory.
Corresponds to
{burd_temp_dir}.

[[burx_transcript]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burx_transcript}::
Absolute path to the transcript file.
Corresponds to
{burd_transcript}.

[[burx_log_hist]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_path
{burx_log_hist}::
Absolute path to the historical log file.
Corresponds to
{burd_log_hist}.

[[burx_label]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_optional
{burx_label}::
Correlation label from
{bure_label}.
Always present — empty string when
{bure_label}
is not set.
Format and interpretation are opaque to BUD.

[[burx_exit_status]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_integer axd_optional
{burx_exit_status}::
Unix exit code of the dispatched command.
Absence indicates the dispatch is still running.

[[burx_ended_at]]
//axl_voices axrg_variable axtu_string axd_optional
{burx_ended_at}::
Nanosecond-precision timestamp when dispatch completed.
Same format as
{burx_began_at}.
Present only when
{burx_exit_status}
is present.

= Bash Utilities Specification
:doctype: book

// tag::mapping-section[]
//
// Category declarations:
// bust_:  Tabtarget (launcher naming vesture)
// busd:   Dispatch (non-terminal, tabtarget system umbrella)
// busdc_: Dispatch Colophon (routing identifier)
// busdf_: Dispatch Frontispiece (human-readable description)
// busdi_: Dispatch Imprint (embedded parameter/target specifier)
// busdm_: Dispatch Formulary (routing component)
// busdl_: Dispatch Launcher (bootstrap script)
// bukro_: Kit Regime Operation (kindle/validate/render)
// burc_:  Configuration Regime (project-level settings)
// burs_:  Station Regime (developer-level settings)
// burd_:  Dispatch Runtime (ephemeral state assembled during dispatch)
// bure_:  Environment Regime (ambient caller-supplied behavioral overrides)
// busi:   Invocation (test invocation umbrella)
// busit_: Invocation Tier (tier classification)
// busig_: Invocation Global (capture state set by zbuto_invoke)
// busx_:  Case Boundary (test case isolation runner)
// busff_: Fact-File (inter-step communication via output files)
// buf_:   Fact Module (dual-write fact-file primitives)
// busy_:  Yelp (pre-rendered formatted string capture)
// burx_:  Exchange Regime (cross-project dispatch state)
//
// Variant patterns:
//   _s: plural form
//   _p: possessive form
//

// Tabtarget Vesture
:bust_tabtarget:        <<bust_tabtarget,Tabtarget Vesture>>

// Dispatch
:busdc_colophon:        <<busdc_colophon,Colophon>>
:busdc_colophon_s:      <<busdc_colophon,Colophons>>
:busdf_frontispiece:    <<busdf_frontispiece,Frontispiece>>
:busdf_frontispiece_s:  <<busdf_frontispiece,Frontispieces>>
:busdi_imprint:         <<busdi_imprint,Imprint>>
